decomposition.title: RFP - Project Polaris: Evaluation of Hosting Options (Cloud, DCaaS & Hybrid)
decomposition.industry: Diversified manufacturing, enterprise solutions, hospitality, digital innovation, and enterprise IT infrastructure
decomposition.estimated_deal_size_usd: No explicit budget stated. Inferred future enterprise hosting/DCaaS/hybrid opportunity approximately $15M-$50M+ over a likely 3-5 year horizon based on scope covering data center exit evaluation, enterprise SAP and non-SAP workloads, VMware estate, physical/virtual Windows and Linux servers, databases, networking, backup/DR, IAM, monitoring/logging/security, and manufacturing integrations. Immediate Phase 0 advisory/evaluation component would likely be materially smaller.
decomposition.contract_duration: Not explicitly stated. The RFP is a Phase 0 evaluation exercise with subsequent phases after vendor selection; inferred future hosting/business-case horizon likely 3-5 years, while the immediate Phase 0 evaluation appears tied to the RFP cycle from 29 May to supplier presentations in the week of 6 July 2026.

requirements.text: Vendors are requested to submit proposals detailing target architecture and platform capabilities.
Vendors are requested to submit proposals detailing a high-level transition approach and indicative sequencing.
Vendors are requested to submit proposals detailing an operating model and support framework.
Vendors are requested to submit proposals detailing commercial and pricing structure.
Proposed solutions should support enterprise-scale deployments, secure connectivity, and integration with Kohler’s enterprise and manufacturing ecosystem.
The evaluation should ensure application performance and availability are maintained or improved.
The evaluation should ensure integration with manufacturing and enterprise systems is supported.
The evaluation should ensure transition risks and business disruption are minimized.
Detailed requirements, response structure, and evaluation inputs are provided in the Evaluation Template (Excel) shared as part of this RFP.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: The detailed requirements, response structure, and evaluation inputs are not in the main document but in a separate Excel template, which could contain mandatory response obligations.

requirements.text: Vendors must clearly articulate the proposed solution covering target-state architecture aligned to multi-account / multi-subscription landing zone principles.
Vendors must clearly articulate the proposed solution covering the landing zone deployment model and application deployment model.
Vendors must clearly articulate the proposed solution covering environment segregation for Prod / Test / Dev.
Vendors must clearly articulate the proposed solution covering identity integration with enterprise identity providers.
Vendors must clearly articulate the proposed solution covering hybrid connectivity approach.
Vendors must clearly articulate the proposed solution covering region strategy and data residency approach.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Data residency is a common hidden compliance risk in hosting RFPs; here it is included within the architecture scope rather than a standalone legal section.
requirements.jurisdiction_note: Region strategy and data residency approach must be addressed, but no specific permitted or prohibited locations are stated.

requirements.text: Vendors must clearly articulate the proposed solution covering disaster recovery, high availability and scalability patterns.
Architecture diagrams, reference designs and supporting documentation are strongly encouraged.
Vendors must respond for relevant scenarios, including Cloud, DCaaS, and Hybrid where applicable, and clearly state assumptions.
In-scope services include enterprise applications currently hosted in the Kohler Wisconsin Data Center.
requirements.jurisdiction_note: Current in-scope data center is identified as the Kohler Wisconsin Data Center.

requirements.text: In-scope services include COTS and internally developed applications, Windows and Linux virtual machines, databases including SQL Server, Oracle, SAP HANA, MySQL, PostgreSQL and other supported platforms, middleware and integration platforms, file storage systems, backup and disaster recovery, IAM integrations, network connectivity, monitoring, logging, and security tooling.
Selected OT-adjacent workloads are in scope only to the extent they are limited to read-only or controlled integration scenarios.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: OT-adjacent workload scope is constrained to read-only or controlled integration scenarios; vendors proposing broader OT control integration could become non-compliant with scope.

requirements.text: A detailed inventory of in-scope applications and infrastructure was attached in the RFP package online and is provided to support vendor response, solution design and indicative pricing.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: The inventory dataset is external to the provided document and is the required baseline for sizing, solution design, and indicative pricing.

requirements.text: Detailed migration execution and implementation services, including actual workload movement, cutover planning and execution activities, are out of scope and should be factored in when the contract is in place.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Execution services are explicitly out of scope for this Phase 0 response and should not be priced or committed as core scope unless separately identified.

requirements.text: Application refactoring, remediation, or modernization activities such as code changes, re-platforming, and re-architecting are out of scope.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Application modernization and remediation are explicitly out of scope; including them as assumed deliverables could misalign the proposal.

requirements.text: Detailed low-level design and configuration-level architecture beyond high-level solution design and reference architecture are out of scope.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: The RFP seeks high-level solution design only, not LLD or configuration-level architecture.

requirements.text: Operational transition and steady-state managed services delivery commitments, including full-scale IT operations and support models, are out of scope.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Steady-state managed services delivery commitments are explicitly out of scope despite pricing guidelines requesting any managed services costs.

requirements.text: Vendors must demonstrate capability to host workloads in compliance with applicable industry/regional standards. Evidence of certifications required.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: This is a buried mandatory compliance evidence requirement in Section 2.4, and the specific certifications are not listed in the main RFP.
requirements.jurisdiction_note: Applicable industry/regional standards are referenced but not specified.
requirements.cert_required: Evidence of certifications required; specific certifications are not named in the provided document.

requirements.text: DCaaS providers must explicitly provide compliance attestations as per industry standards.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: This applies specifically to DCaaS providers and points to a separate Vendor response template for more information, creating a hidden compliance-response dependency.
requirements.cert_required: Compliance attestations as per industry standards for DCaaS providers.

requirements.text: Vendors are expected to review, validate, and clearly state any deviations or additional assumptions in their proposal.
Vendors are expected to base their response on the provided inventory dataset and use it as the baseline for sizing, solution design, and indicative pricing.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: The inventory is the mandated basis for sizing, solution design, and indicative pricing, but the landscape may change due to onboarding and offboarding activities.

requirements.text: Vendors are expected to highlight any data gaps or assumptions made due to incomplete information.
Where data gaps exist, vendors must clearly state assumptions used for sizing and costing.
Responses must be complete, clearly structured, and aligned to each requirement.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Incomplete or unstructured responses may fail comparability expectations.

requirements.text: Vendors must clearly state all assumptions, dependencies, and constraints associated with their response.
Any optional services or additional capabilities not covered under the scope must be explicitly identified separately.
Proposals must be submitted within the defined timeline via the designated communication channel.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Late or misdirected submission is a major compliance risk, although the RFP does not explicitly say late submissions will be rejected.

requirements.text: Vendors are required to complete the response template in detail; simple ‘Yes/No’ answers will not be considered sufficient.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: This is a response-format trap: simple Yes/No answers are expressly insufficient, so narrative detail is required throughout the template.

requirements.text: Vendors must provide high-level indicative solution designs and supporting explanations where relevant.
All clarification queries must be submitted within the defined timeline and use the template for Q&A provided to the contact provided.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Clarification queries must use the provided Q&A template and contact route; missing the timeline can eliminate the chance to clarify assumptions.

requirements.text: Vendors are required to submit their responses using the Vendor Response template provided as part of this RFP to ensure consistency and comparability across all submissions.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: The required Vendor Response template is external to the provided document and may contain mandatory fields.

requirements.text: Pricing must be transparent, structured and clearly itemized, aligned to the requirements of this RFP and the inventory baseline provided.

Vendors must provide comprehensive cost breakup, including foundational setup costs, application and infrastructure hosting costs, migration or implementation costs if applicable, and any consulting, support, or managed services costs.
Vendors must provide separate cost views for SAP and non-SAP workloads, ensuring clear visibility into cost drivers across application categories.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Failure to separate SAP and non-SAP economics would reduce required cost-driver visibility.

requirements.text: All commercial values and pricing submissions must be quoted in United States Dollars (USD).
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Currency compliance is mandatory and easy to miss for global bidders pricing in local currency.
requirements.jurisdiction_note: All commercial values must be in United States Dollars.

requirements.text: Proposals should include cost increase protections and efficiency gains that lead to a reduction or no more than a reasonable increase over time, based on prices being paid and not general discounts off list price.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Kohler requires commercial protections tied to actual prices paid, not only list-price discounts.

requirements.text: Anything less than net 90 payment terms will not be considered competitive, and the proposal should contemplate no less than net 90 payment terms.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Commercial near-disqualifier: payment terms below net 90 are expressly stated as not competitive, though not explicitly rejected.

requirements.text: Vendors are expected to provide a high-level overview of their proposed program governance model aligned to the solution being proposed.

The proposed governance overview should include indicative governance structure and engagement model, reporting and review mechanisms, escalation framework, and high-level key roles and responsibilities.
All information shared as part of this RFP is confidential and must be treated accordingly by participating vendors.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: The confidentiality requirement is in the appendix and applies to all information shared as part of the RFP.

requirements.text: Vendors must ensure that all information received from Kohler is used solely for the purpose of responding to this RFP and must not disclose, distribute, or share any information with third parties without prior written consent from Kohler.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: The restriction may affect use of subcontractors, partners, hyperscalers, or third-party estimators unless prior written consent is obtained.

requirements.text: Vendors are expected to ensure that their proposed solutions are capable of complying with applicable industry standards, regulatory requirements and data protection obligations relevant to enterprise IT and manufacturing environments.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: This appendix requirement restates broad compliance capability for enterprise IT and manufacturing environments; detailed obligations are deferred to later phases.
requirements.jurisdiction_note: Applicable industry standards, regulatory requirements, and data protection obligations are not specifically listed.

ambiguities: The Evaluation Template (Excel) is repeatedly referenced as containing detailed requirements, scoring structure, indicative weightages, compliance details, and vendor response expectations, but it is not included in the provided document. Mandatory criteria may exist there and must be reviewed separately.
The Vendor Response template is required and referenced for additional compliance-attestation information, but it is not included in the provided document.
The inventory baseline spreadsheet with counts and sizing is referenced but not included, preventing precise deal-size calculation and workload quantification.
No explicit contract term, renewal period, or hosting service duration is stated. The document says detailed contractual terms will be finalized in subsequent phases.
No explicit budget, current spend, not-to-exceed value, or pricing ceiling is stated. Deal size is inferred from enterprise hosting scope only.
The statement that payment terms below net 90 'will not be considered competitive' is commercially severe, but the document does not explicitly say such proposals will be rejected or disqualified.
The phrase 'Evidence of certifications required' does not identify which certifications are mandatory, creating a compliance-response ambiguity.

The RFP is described as a Phase 0 evaluation exercise and not a commitment to a target architecture or execution approach, yet pricing asks for hosting, migration/implementation, consulting, support, and managed services costs. The intended commercial scope for this response is therefore partly ambiguous.
compliance_red_flags: [PAGE 7] Vendors must demonstrate capability to host workloads in compliance with applicable industry/regional standards, and evidence of certifications is required, but specific certifications are not named.
[PAGE 7] DCaaS providers must explicitly provide compliance attestations as per industry standards, with more information in a separate Vendor response template not included in the document.

[PAGE 9] The Evaluation Template (Excel), not included in the provided text, contains detailed evaluation criteria, sub-categories, scoring structure, response assessment model, and indicative weightages.

[PAGE 10] Payment terms below net 90 are stated as not competitive; this may materially weaken or effectively exclude a proposal commercially even though not framed as an explicit rejection condition.

[PAGE 11] Vendors must not disclose, distribute, or share Kohler information with third parties without prior written consent, which may affect subcontractor, hyperscaler, tooling, or partner solution development workflows.
[PAGE 11] Detailed legal terms, contractual conditions, compliance requirements, and service-level agreements are deferred to later phases, creating downstream acceptance risk.

similar_deals.title: Kohler Project Polaris Cloud Migration / Hybrid Data Center Exit / Hosting Options Evaluation Phase 0
similar_deals.outcome: Internal past proposal record; award outcome not provided in supplied intelligence
similar_deals.why_relevant: Exact client/RFP match covering Kohler, Wisconsin Data Center, global estate, SAP and VMware workloads, cloud/DCaaS/hybrid evaluation, operating model, transition approach, TCO, and Phase 0 advisory scope.
similar_deals.key_lesson: The strongest internal proposal pattern is a three-month Hosting Decision Lab with workload baselining, SAP/VMware dependency mapping, RTO/RPO, IAM, security, observability, contracts, run costs, placement scorecards, landing-zone blueprints, migration waves, rollback gates, vendor-coexistence RACI, FinOps, and open-book USD TCO.

similar_deals.title: Global Manufacturing Hybrid Hosting / Data-Center Exit Phase-0 Advisory
similar_deals.deal_size_usd: $279,533 fixed price, 12 weeks, with 20% fees at risk
similar_deals.outcome: Internal past proposal record; award outcome not provided in supplied intelligence
similar_deals.why_relevant: Strong match to Kohler’s Phase 0 evaluation need, including enterprise platforms, plants, hospitality, analytics, connected products, DCaaS/hybrid options, target architecture, operating model, and transition sequencing.
similar_deals.key_lesson: A lean fixed-price Phase 0 package with fee-at-risk, open-book TCO, FinOps guardrails, Wisconsin Data Center transition cell, rollback gates, and vendor-coexistence RACI is likely the most price-disciplined posture for the immediate advisory phase.

similar_deals.title: SAP S/4HANA Global Rollout
similar_deals.why_relevant: Relevant to Kohler because Polaris includes SAP enterprise workloads, global manufacturing operations, multi-region delivery needs, availability protection, and future hosting/transformation potential.
similar_deals.key_lesson: Won through SAP/manufacturing credentials, zero-downtime methodology, global follow-the-sun delivery, executive sponsor alignment, and named client references.

similar_deals.title: Manufacturing IT Transformation
similar_deals.why_relevant: Comparable manufacturing IT transformation size within the inferred $15M-$50M+ downstream range and relevant to transition-heavy hosting modernization.
similar_deals.key_lesson: Won on fastest mobilization, with team ready within two weeks, plus manufacturing domain credentials. For Polaris, named team readiness and rapid Phase 0 mobilization should be explicit.

similar_deals.title: Manufacturing IT Transformation
similar_deals.why_relevant: Closest cautionary loss in manufacturing IT transformation and within Kohler’s inferred downstream deal range; directly informs compliance and appendix discipline.
similar_deals.key_lesson: Lost due to a missed local entity requirement in a compliance appendix. For Kohler, the Excel Evaluation Template, legal/entity requirements, data residency, support location, and operational obligations must be treated as gating items.

similar_deals.title: Telecom IT Transformation
similar_deals.why_relevant: Industry is less similar, but the pricing lesson is highly relevant because Polaris has no explicit budget and internal Phase 0 price points vary significantly.
similar_deals.key_lesson: Lost because pricing was 20% above the winning bid despite a stronger solution. For Polaris, keep Phase 0 pricing transparent and separate advisory, transition, run, and optional downstream costs.

win_intel.win_probability_rationale: Estimated win probability is 47% with medium confidence. Positive evidence: internal proposal intelligence contains an exact Kohler Project Polaris match, including tested solution patterns, pricing options of $279,533 and $595,000 for Phase 0, and a downstream $30.667M hybrid modernization model aligned to the inferred $15M-$50M+ opportunity. Deal corpus evidence also shows relevant manufacturing wins: DEAL-003 won a $71M SAP/manufacturing/global rollout, DEAL-089 won a $22M manufacturing IT transformation partly through rapid mobilization, and DEAL-038/DEAL-094 indicate manufacturing buyers reward domain depth and risk-reducing IP. Negative evidence: there is no public procurement award data to validate competitor pricing or incumbent win patterns, so confidence cannot be high. There is also no supplied evidence of a prior Kohler win, no confirmed incumbent displacement position, and the Evaluation Template is unavailable. Cautionary losses are material: DEAL-056 shows a manufacturing IT bid lost due to a missed appendix/local entity requirement, and DEAL-029 shows a better technical solution lost when priced 20% above the winner. Because procurement data is absent and the immediate RFP is a Phase 0 evaluation likely involving incumbents and multiple advisory/hosting competitors, the probability is kept below 50% despite strong internal fit.
capability_gaps: Evaluation Template gap: the RFP states detailed requirements, response structure, and evaluation inputs are in the shared Excel Evaluation Template, but that template content is not available in the provided intelligence. This creates a compliance risk until every worksheet/question is mapped and answered.

Evidence gap for multi-account/multi-subscription landing zone: the RFP requires target-state architecture aligned to landing-zone principles, environment segregation for Prod/Test/Dev, IAM integration, hybrid connectivity, and region/data residency strategy. Past proposal patterns contain strong language, but the bid must include Kohler-specific diagrams, control mapping, and assumptions rather than generic architecture claims.

Manufacturing ecosystem integration evidence gap: the RFP requires integration with Kohler’s manufacturing and enterprise systems. The intelligence supports manufacturing positioning, but the bid must prove plant/edge, OT/IT boundary, latency, SAP/non-SAP dependency, and site connectivity handling for Kohler-specific workloads.

Performance and availability proof gap: the RFP requires application performance and availability to be maintained or improved. Prior proposal content offers 20% fee-at-risk and rollback gates, but the team must establish current baselines, RTO/RPO tiers, monitoring/logging approach, DR test plan, and measurable success criteria.

Commercial validation gap: no public procurement award data is available to benchmark competitor pricing. Internal Phase 0 pricing ranges from $279,533 to $595,000 and downstream pricing around $30.667M, but market competitiveness is not externally validated.
recommended_win_themes: Vendor-neutral Hosting Decision Lab: position Polaris as a Kohler-owned decision package across public cloud, DCaaS, retained data center, hybrid, plant/edge, and SaaS integration—not a forced cloud migration.

Manufacturing-continuity first: emphasize protection of plants, SAP and non-SAP workloads, hospitality operations, connected products, network dependencies, RTO/RPO, backup/DR, IAM, monitoring, and business continuity during transition.

Open-book economics and FinOps: provide transparent USD-only TCO scenarios separating Phase 0 advisory, migration/transformation cost, hosting/DCaaS run cost, managed services, licensing, assumptions, exclusions, and optional downstream waves.

Low-risk transition with accountable governance: use migration sequencing, rollback gates, manufacturing hypercare, vendor-coexistence RACI, onsite Wisconsin Data Center transition cell, and fee-at-risk tied to maintaining or improving performance and availability.

Proven global manufacturing transformation capability: cite relevant won deal patterns around SAP/manufacturing/global delivery, rapid mobilization, proprietary risk-reduction IP, and follow-the-sun support without overstating Kohler-specific win history.
risk_factors: No public procurement data is available, so competitor pricing and award patterns cannot be externally validated. This reduces confidence and argues against an optimistic win probability.

Incumbent-defended risk is implied by prior Polaris proposal patterns referencing vendor/Cognizant coexistence. If an incumbent already controls infrastructure, tooling, or institutional knowledge, displacement probability is lower unless the proposal is explicitly coexistence-friendly.

Pricing risk: DEAL-029 shows a technically stronger bid lost when priced 20% above the winner. Internal Phase 0 prices vary materially from $279,533 to $595,000; choosing the higher option without clear added value may weaken competitiveness.
Compliance/detail risk: DEAL-056 was lost due to a missed local entity requirement in a compliance appendix. The unavailable Evaluation Template and appendices are bid-critical.

Generic cloud-migration risk: Kohler is asking for evaluation of cloud, DCaaS, and hybrid hosting options. A cloud-first or hyperscaler-first response would conflict with the stated evaluation objective.

Capability proof risk: the proposal must demonstrate SAP, VMware, physical/virtual Windows and Linux, databases, networking, backup/DR, IAM, monitoring/logging/security, and manufacturing integration coverage; unsupported claims will reduce credibility.
must_fix_before_bidding: Obtain and complete a line-by-line compliance matrix against the Evaluation Template Excel, including all appendices, legal/entity requirements, data residency, security, insurance, subcontractor, support-location, and transition obligations.

Select a disciplined Phase 0 price point and avoid making the advisory phase appear like a disguised transformation sale. Evidence supports a lean fixed-price 12-week option around $279,533 and a more robust option around $595,000; recommend leading with the lower/lean option plus clearly optional extensions.

Produce Kohler-specific target architecture artifacts: cloud/DCaaS/hybrid option views, multi-account or multi-subscription landing zone, network and hybrid connectivity model, IAM integration, Prod/Test/Dev segregation, region/data residency model, backup/DR, observability, and security controls.

Name the delivery team, mobilization timeline, Wisconsin Data Center/site engagement model, executive sponsor, and manufacturing/SAP/hybrid-cloud SMEs. DEAL-089 shows fastest mobilization was a win factor; DEAL-003 shows named references and executive alignment mattered.

Include quantified risk-reduction mechanisms: workload-by-workload placement scorecards, open-book USD TCO, FinOps assumptions, rollback gates, manufacturing hypercare, DR test blueprint, vendor/coexistence RACI, and measurable performance/availability baselines.

competitors.likelihood_to_bid: medium; confidence: medium. Accenture is capability-aligned and has strong managed-services demand, but high likelihood is not justified because the provided job-posting intelligence found no Accenture hiring specifically in Kohler/Wisconsin for this skill set and no public Accenture-Kohler data-center award. Evidence: Q3 FY2026 managed-services bookings $9.06B and revenue $9.39B; source: Accenture Q3 FY2026 earnings release. No recent contract wins in Wisconsin/Kohler geography were provided.
competitors.predicted_positioning: Likely angle: 'end-to-end reinvention of Kohler's digital core' covering cloud, infrastructure, SAP, cybersecurity, network intelligence, manufacturing/Industry X, and data-center strategy. Evidence: Digital Core includes technology strategy, architecture, data/AI, app modernization/management, infrastructure, data and cloud; source: Accenture Reinvention Services announcement, Mar. 13, 2026. SAP angle likely strengthened by Accenture's SAP forward-deployed engineering program for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private and SAP Business AI Platform; source: Accenture SAP announcement, Jun. 8, 2026. Data-center/facility credibility likely based on DLB acquisition; source: Accenture DLB announcement.
competitors.predicted_price_range_usd: No public procurement price anchor available; public contract data explicitly unavailable. Margin-grounded estimate only. Accenture operating margin = 17.0%, so price floor = delivery cost / 0.83 = 1.205x delivery cost. Revenue/employee was unavailable in the provided sources, so no revenue-per-employee calculation is used. For the user-stated future enterprise hosting/DCaaS/hybrid envelope of roughly $15M-$50M over 3-5 years, Accenture is expected to bid in the upper band: Phase 0 advisory roughly $900K-$2.5M; full 3-5 year transformation/run option roughly $25M-$60M, because their 17% margin floor plus premium consulting positioning makes sub-market pricing unlikely. Confidence: medium; source: Accenture Q3 FY2026 earnings release; no public contract awards provided.
their_strengths: Scale in consulting and managed services: Q3 FY2026 total new bookings $19.32B, consulting bookings $10.26B, managed-services bookings $9.06B; source: Accenture Q3 FY2026 earnings release, Jun. 18, 2026.
Managed-services fit: Q3 FY2026 managed-services revenue $9.39B, up 8% USD / 5% local currency YoY; source: Accenture Q3 FY2026 earnings release.

Data-center advisory/facility capability expanded via 65% majority stake in DLB Associates, adding approximately 620 employees with site selection, due diligence, design engineering, commissioning, construction quality and energy-optimization expertise; source: Accenture DLB announcement.
SAP relevance: launched forward-deployed engineering program with SAP involving SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private and SAP Business AI Platform; source: Accenture announcement, Jun. 8, 2026.
Cloud/security relevance: expanded Google Cloud security partnership for AI-driven cyber threats and cloud security; source: Accenture announcement, Mar. 11, 2026.
their_weaknesses: No public Accenture-Kohler data-center deal found; source: provided web intelligence.
No Accenture job posting was found specifically tied to Kohler, Wisconsin or the Kohler Wisconsin Data Center; source: provided job-posting intelligence.
Premium-cost risk: 17.0% operating margin implies floor pricing of cost / 0.83 = 1.205x cost, before any strategic premium; source: Accenture Q3 FY2026 earnings release.

Their broad reinvention story may over-scope Phase 0 beyond Kohler's need for an objective hosting-options evaluation; source basis: Accenture's broad Digital Core/Reinvention Services positioning, Mar. 13, 2026.
No recent contract wins in Wisconsin/Kohler geography were provided; source: public contract awards section states no procurement data available.
how_to_beat_them: Force a Phase 0 deliverable that is implementation-neutral and Kohler-owned: Accenture will likely try to expand the advisory into a broad 'reinvention' / managed-services program; require workload-by-workload disposition, unit-price migration catalog, and exit-ready runbooks before any run award. It remains above the $1M enterprise minimum and inside the lower part of the competitor price-to-win range, but carries higher risk around transition disruption, performance baselining, manufacturing integration proof, and completeness of SAP/VMware/DC-exit execution.

Managed services are monthly recurring. Infrastructure, cloud/DCaaS and licensing are open-book pass-through allowances with FinOps true-up after discovery. pricing.competitive_rationale: Baseline is HCLTech's Kohler-specific Project Polaris pricing pattern: Phase 0 advisory at $279,533-$595,000 and a 36-month downstream co-managed hybrid modernization/control tower at $30,667,392. No public contract award benchmarks were available, so market validation is limited. The recommended $30.667M full-program price sits within the provided competitor price-to-win range of $18M-$36M for a 3-5 year transformation/run program, above aggressive DXC/Kyndryl-style floor pricing and below a premium Accenture/TCS-style approach. Live Azure/AWS unit pricing was unavailable in the supplied pricing feed, so infrastructure is treated as a scaled enterprise cloud/DCaaS/licensing pass-through allowance to be reconciled in Phase 0 through open-book FinOps; confidence is reduced accordingly.

draft.executive_summary: Kohler's Project Polaris should not be treated as a binary cloud-versus-data-center exercise. The July 2026 CTO profile in The Org identifies priorities to use digital and data to simplify processes, deepen customer engagement and spark innovation. Cognizant's January 2026 investor material points to operational and business excellence through technology platforms and digital engineering, while Microsoft customer material circa 2023 highlights connected-product customer-data protection as a visible trust commitment. Those priorities make hosting a business decision as much as an infrastructure decision. HCLTech proposes a fixed-fee, vendor-neutral Polaris Decision Digital Twin inside a 12-week Hosting Decision Lab. The Digital Twin will baseline SAP and non-SAP workloads, VMware and physical estates, databases, network paths, IAM, logging, backup, disaster recovery controls, manufacturing integrations, hospitality-critical systems and connected-product dependencies. It will compare public cloud, DCaaS, retained Wisconsin Data Center, hybrid, plant/edge and SaaS integration options workload by workload. Kohler receives open-book USD economics, binding migration and run unit prices, target-state architecture, risk controls, vendor-coexistence governance and a contractual no-lock-in clause that allows execution to be awarded to any provider. This approach complements Kohler's Cognizant-led AI, cloud and operations ecosystem rather than forcing a clean-slate replacement. Polaris becomes a Kohler-owned hosting decision engine: evidence-led, manufacturing-continuity first, security-by-design and commercially transparent. Leadership can decide with confidence while plants, SAP, hospitality operations and customer-facing digital experiences continue to perform.
win_themes: Vendor-neutral Hosting Decision Lab across public cloud, DCaaS, retained data center, hybrid, plant/edge and SaaS integration.
Manufacturing-continuity first for plants, SAP and non-SAP workloads, hospitality operations, connected products, RTO/RPO, backup/DR, IAM and monitoring.
Open-book economics and FinOps with USD-only TCO separated by advisory, migration, hosting, managed services, licensing, assumptions and optional waves.
Low-risk transition with accountable governance, rollback gates, Wisconsin Data Center transition cell, vendor-coexistence RACI and fee-at-risk governance.
Proven global manufacturing transformation capability using SAP/manufacturing delivery patterns, proprietary risk-reduction IP and follow-the-sun support.

sections.section_title: Target state architecture, security and governance capabilities
sections.content: Kohler needs a target state that can be defended by workload evidence, not by a provider preference. HCLTech will build the Polaris Decision Digital Twin as the architectural source of truth for the Wisconsin Data Center, SAP and non-SAP applications, VMware and VxRail clusters, physical and virtual servers, databases, network dependencies, IAM, logging, backup, disaster recovery and manufacturing or hospitality integrations. Each workload will be scored against public cloud, DCaaS, retained data center, hybrid, plant/edge and SaaS integration scenarios. Security and governance are designed into the scoring model. We will map data classification, identity patterns, privileged access, encryption, key ownership, logging, backup immutability, regional privacy, data-residency, audit and connected-product trust controls to each hosting option. Governance will keep Kohler in control through an evidence register, decision log, assumption ledger, architecture review board and no-lock-in clause. The win theme is vendor neutrality: Kohler owns the architecture evidence and can use it with HCLTech, an incumbent, a hyperscaler, a DCaaS provider or another execution partner. The forward path is a target-state blueprint that protects trust while making every hosting decision explainable.

sections.section_title: Migration approach, risk considerations and transition strategy
sections.content: Kohler's transition risk is not abstract; it sits in plant uptime, SAP availability, network dependencies, hospitality operations and connected-product services that cannot be disrupted by an evaluation. The 12-week Hosting Decision Lab therefore separates decision readiness from execution. We will mobilize a Polaris control tower, establish an onsite Wisconsin Data Center transition cell, define a vendor-coexistence RACI with Cognizant and other incumbents, and confirm discovery access before any migration recommendation is made. The methodology baselines inventory, dependency maps, service ownership, contracts, RTO/RPO, change windows and current performance. Migration waves are then sequenced by business criticality, technical dependency, security readiness, licensing impact, latency sensitivity and rollback feasibility. Every proposed wave will include entry criteria, cutover rehearsal, rollback gate, DR validation, manufacturing hypercare, communications plan and owner accountability. Where commercially selected, HCLTech can align a portion of Phase 0 fees to maintaining or improving application performance and availability during discovery and planning. The win theme is low-risk transition with accountable governance. Polaris moves forward only when Kohler can see the dependency evidence, risk controls and business-continuity protections behind each step.

Hosting placement for Kohler will fail if network behavior is treated as an afterthought. Polaris will model connectivity as a decision criterion from day one, covering Wisconsin Data Center paths, plant and edge links, cloud and DCaaS connectivity, SaaS access, hospitality operations, remote support paths, identity traffic, backup flows, monitoring feeds and connected-product data paths. We will capture latency, bandwidth, packet-loss sensitivity, firewall and segmentation rules, DNS and IP dependencies, egress cost exposure, routing resilience and operational ownership. For public cloud options, the design will evaluate private connectivity such as ExpressRoute or equivalent services where justified; for DCaaS and retained data center options, it will test carrier diversity, cross-connect resilience and coexistence with existing network operations. The Decision Digital Twin will expose which workloads are portable, which are network-bound and which require hybrid patterns because plant, SAP or customer-facing dependencies make a single hosting location impractical. The win theme is manufacturing-continuity first: network design protects production flow before platform preference. The forward result is a connectivity blueprint that allows Kohler to compare hosting scenarios without hiding network cost, latency or resilience risk.

sections.section_title: Availability, resilience and disaster recovery approach
sections.content: Kohler's resilience posture must start with business impact, not a generic platform availability claim. HCLTech will classify workloads into resilience tiers using RTO, RPO, plant criticality, SAP dependency, hospitality impact, connected-product exposure, regulatory evidence needs and recovery complexity. The Decision Digital Twin will map current backup, replication, monitoring, alerting, runbook, dependency and DR-test evidence against each tier. We will then compare cloud-native resilience, DCaaS recovery, retained Wisconsin Data Center continuity, hybrid failover and plant/edge survivability patterns. For each viable hosting option, the output will include recovery architecture, data-protection design, immutable-backup approach, monitoring and escalation model, DR test plan, dependency restart order and residual risk. This section carries the manufacturing-continuity and low-risk transition themes: no target state is acceptable unless it maintains or improves availability for plants, SAP, hospitality operations and customer-facing services. Resilience decisions will also feed the open-book TCO so Kohler sees the cost of each RTO/RPO promise. The forward position is clear: Polaris will recommend only hosting options that can be tested, governed and recovered under real operating conditions.

Kohler should not have to infer the economics behind a hosting recommendation. HCLTech will deliver USD-only, open-book TCO scenarios that separate Phase 0 advisory cost, migration or transformation cost, cloud or DCaaS run cost, retained data center cost, managed services, licensing, network, security, backup, observability, exit assumptions, exclusions and optional downstream waves. The cost model will show workload-level unit prices, business-case assumptions, sensitivity ranges, reserved-capacity or commitment logic where relevant, and FinOps guardrails for chargeback, showback and consumption governance. The knowledge base supports three commercial anchors: a lean 12-week advisory package at $279,533.42, an expanded fixed-fee Hosting Decision Lab at $595,000, and an optional 36-month co-managed hybrid modernization model at $30,667,392, with final selection driven by agreed scope. Our differentiator is binding migration and run unit prices inside the Polaris Decision Digital Twin plus a no-lock-in clause that lets Kohler award execution to any provider. The win theme is open-book economics and FinOps. The forward outcome is a finance-ready decision pack that exposes cost, risk and optionality before Kohler commits capital.

Polaris must define how Kohler will operate the future estate across platforms, providers and regions, not only where workloads will run. HCLTech will design a hybrid operating model that clarifies accountability among Kohler, HCLTech, Cognizant and other incumbents, hyperscalers, DCaaS providers, SaaS owners and plant technology teams. The model will cover incident, problem, change, release, security, backup, DR, access, asset, configuration, FinOps, service-level and executive governance. The onsite Wisconsin Data Center transition cell will give Kohler local coordination during discovery and planning, while HCLTech's global delivery and follow-the-sun support patterns provide coverage for multi-region manufacturing and hospitality operations. We will also define how observability and AIOps integrate with the planned Cognizant Neuro IT Operations direction, so the operating model complements the incumbent ecosystem rather than duplicating it. The win theme is low-risk transition with accountable governance. The forward operating state gives Kohler a single control framework for hybrid hosting, clear service ownership and measurable support outcomes regardless of which execution provider is selected.

Kohler needs tools that turn dispersed infrastructure data into an executable decision, not another static assessment deck. The Polaris Decision Digital Twin will combine validated inventory, dependency mapping, workload scoring, security control mapping, RTO/RPO tiers, network paths, cost assumptions, migration sequencing and operating-model accountabilities. HCLTech will apply established risk-reduction IP for dependency validation, migration-wave scoring, cutover readiness and executive decision packaging, supported by global manufacturing transformation patterns around SAP, infrastructure modernization and follow-the-sun operations. The tool ecosystem will remain open: it can incorporate existing CMDB, monitoring, security, backup, cloud, DCaaS and service-management evidence rather than forcing a proprietary replacement. Outputs will be usable by Kohler, HCLTech, Cognizant, hyperscalers, DCaaS providers or future bidders. This section reinforces the vendor-neutral Hosting Decision Lab theme. Platform maturity is demonstrated by the ability to compare retained data center, public cloud, hybrid, plant/edge and SaaS options using the same evidence model. The forward result is an auditable digital asset Kohler can keep refreshing as workloads, providers and business priorities change.

HCLTech will align Polaris outputs directly to the RFP's expected deliverables so Kohler receives a decision package, not disconnected work products. Deliverables will include the current-state evidence register; workload placement scorecards; public cloud, DCaaS, retained data center, hybrid, plant/edge and SaaS scenario comparison; target-state architecture; security and governance matrix; networking and connectivity blueprint; resilience and DR design; open-book USD TCO and FinOps model; migration wave plan; rollback and hypercare model; vendor-coexistence RACI; compliance and audit evidence matrix; operating model; implementation-ready downstream RFP; and executive decision pack. Acceptance criteria will be measurable: validated inventory coverage, signed assumptions, traceable scoring, cost-model transparency, risk disposition, RTO/RPO mapping, governance ownership and decision readiness. Each deliverable will identify the owner, input source, review forum and executive use case. The win themes converge here: Kohler owns the evidence, protects manufacturing continuity, sees transparent economics and can transition with governed risk. The forward deliverable is a board-ready Polaris package that lets Kohler choose the right hosting path without surrendering optionality.

pursuit_plan.strategy: Position the response as a decision-quality Phase 0 advisory engagement that gives Kohler a defensible business case and architecture path across Cloud, DCaaS, and Hybrid options. Do not overcommit to a single platform before inventory validation; instead, demonstrate superior methodology, reference architectures, transparent economics, and a low-disruption transition path for SAP, VMware, databases, and manufacturing integrations. The pursuit should aim to win the Phase 0 evaluation while shaping the criteria and roadmap for the larger 3-5 year hosting and managed services opportunity.
pursuit_plan.win_strategy: Win by owning the Phase 0 decision framework: provide Kohler with the clearest, most credible comparison of Cloud, DCaaS, and Hybrid options tied to quantified TCO, operational risk, SAP/manufacturing constraints, and an executable transition roadmap.
pursuit_plan.pricing_approach: Use a hybrid pricing strategy: fixed-fee or capped-fee pricing for the immediate Phase 0 evaluation, combined with transparent scenario-based TCO models for future Cloud, DCaaS, and Hybrid run/transition options. Because evaluation weights are unknown but cost transparency is explicitly listed, price competitively with clear assumptions, unit rates, pass-through cloud/DCaaS costs, managed service fees, migration bands, support tiers, and sensitivity ranges rather than a single opaque value. Build in net-90 working-capital impact, separate advisory from implementation/run costs, and show value through risk-adjusted TCO, avoided data center costs, improved resilience, and optionality for SAP/manufacturing workloads.
key_risks: The Evaluation Template, Vendor Response Template, and inventory baseline are missing; hidden mandatory criteria, scoring weights, certification requirements, and workload quantities may materially change the pursuit strategy.

The RFP asks for Phase 0 evaluation but also requests indicative pricing for hosting, migration, implementation, consulting, support, and managed services; scope boundaries must be explicit to avoid underpricing or overcommitting.
No explicit budget, contract term, baseline spend, or workload sizing is provided, making pricing accuracy and TCO comparison risky unless assumptions and sensitivity ranges are clearly documented.
Kohler’s SAP HANA, Oracle, SQL Server, VMware, and database licensing positions may materially affect cloud/DCaaS economics and migration feasibility.
Manufacturing and OT-adjacent integrations create business-disruption risk; latency, availability, maintenance windows, and controlled/read-only integration boundaries must be treated carefully.
Data residency and regional hosting requirements are not fully defined despite global operations; architecture must support regional options and governance flexibility.
Network connectivity and DR requirements are central evaluation areas but lack quantified RTO/RPO, bandwidth, latency, and dependency data in the provided summary.
Commercial terms are severe: net-90 payment expectations may affect working capital and must be built into pricing assumptions.

Competitors may compete aggressively with low-cost offshore managed services or incumbent infrastructure relationships; proposal must differentiate beyond price through decision quality, risk reduction, and implementation credibility.
VMware/Broadcom licensing changes and private-cloud economics may shift the attractiveness of DCaaS versus hyperscaler or native-cloud options during the evaluation.

gaps: Competitor intelligence is materially incomplete in the visible output: only Accenture is shown, while Cognizant appears to be a critical incumbent/near-incumbent given the Jan 2026 enterprise IT/cloud/operations renewal signal in client intel.
Competitor pricing is weakly grounded. Accenture range is broad and mostly margin-inferred, with no comparable advisory/DCaaS/cloud migration procurement anchors or delivery-unit assumptions.

No visible competitor vulnerabilities or killer differentiator across the competitive field. Without Cognizant/Kyndryl/HCL/TCS/Infosys/DXC/IBM-style analysis, the proposal may miss the real buying dynamics.

WIN_INTEL has useful internal similar-deal patterns, but the visible output is truncated and does not show an explicit calibrated win probability, capability gaps, disqualifier assessment, or recommended win strategy.
The strongest WIN_INTEL records include exact/current-RFP internal proposal records with no award outcome, which is useful but not enough by itself for probability calibration.
Client intel is mostly strong, but some signals are recent/future-dated and should be treated carefully unless sources were verified; still, this is not a blocker.
strengths: Client intel contains real, specific signals rather than generic industry trends, especially the Cognizant renewal, connected-product expansion, Energy carve-out complexity, Microsoft security posture, and AIOps/Neuro IT Operations signal.
Unstated needs are largely actionable: workload placement, hybrid operating model, observability/AIOps, security-by-design, provider governance, transition risk, resilience, and FinOps.

WIN_INTEL includes useful internal proposal patterns for a 12-week/3-month Phase 0 Hosting Decision Lab, including TCO, placement scorecards, dependency mapping, migration waves, FinOps, rollback gates, and vendor-coexistence RACI.
Accenture competitor write-up uses some concrete public evidence around bookings, managed services, SAP, and data-center capability expansion, even though the field is incomplete.

agentic_decisions.decision: Identified 6 competitors autonomously: ['Accenture', 'Kyndryl', 'HCLTech', 'Tata Consultancy Services', 'Infosys', 'DXC Technology']
agentic_decisions.reasoning: Position the response as a decision-quality Phase 0 advisory engagement that gives Kohler a defensible business case and architecture path across Cloud, DCaaS, and Hybrid options. Do not overcommit to a single platform before inventory validation; instead, demonstrate superior methodology, reference architectures, transparent economics, and a low-disruption transition path for SAP, VMware, databases, and manufacturing integrations. The pursuit should aim to win the Phase 0 evaluation while shaping the criteria and roadmap for the larger 3-5 year hosting and managed services opportunity.

Client intelligence is good enough to proceed: it contains specific Kohler signals around Cognizant renewal, cloud/IT operations, AIOps, connected products, security, carve-out complexity, and governance needs. WIN_INTEL also has useful internal analogs and practical Phase 0 lessons. However, the competitor analysis has a material blind spot: Cognizant is strongly signaled as an incumbent enterprise IT/cloud/operations partner and should likely be treated as the primary competitive threat, not omitted or buried. For a hosting/DCaaS/hybrid evaluation, the competitive field must include incumbent/coexistence dynamics and likely infrastructure outsourcing players. Current Accenture pricing is too broad and only loosely evidence-based. Because this weakness directly affects positioning, pricing, and win narrative, the outputs are not yet good enough for a winning proposal without targeted re-run/deepening.

Current visible competitor output is materially incomplete and appears to miss the most important incumbent signal: Kohler's renewed Cognizant enterprise IT/cloud/operations partnership. This could cause the proposal to misposition against the real buyer concern: whether to extend/benchmark the incumbent versus use an independent evaluator or transition partner.

Only two options are shown. The required three-option structure is incomplete, and there is no lean/Phase 0-only alternative despite the RFP being an immediate Phase 0 evaluation.; Magnitude is broadly acceptable for a full 36-month transformation/run deal: the recommended $30.67M sits inside the $18M-$36M competitor price-to-win range and within the inferred $15M-$50M+ opportunity. However, it may overrepresent the immediate RFP if Kohler is only buying Phase 0 first.; Cost layers are mostly present: people, infrastructure, migration, governance, contingency and margin are included. However, infrastructure is a broad allowance and explicitly says live cloud pricing was unavailable, which does not satisfy the desired use of real cloud pricing data.; Internal math is mostly consistent: annual cost times 3 years matches total cost, and margins of 18%-20% are realistic. Premium is greater than balanced. But the missing lean option prevents full option differentiation validation.; The recommended option is market-calibrated to the stated $18M-$36M full-program price-to-win band and includes a Phase 0 amount within the $650K-$1.25M range. The premium option is above the price-to-win range but is clearly marked non-recommended.; The output cites a very specific HCLTech historical pricing anchor that is not supported by the supplied market data. This is a material credibility issue unless that benchmark was available from another validated source.; No explicit confidence level is provided, despite limited market data and missing workload inventory. Confidence honesty check is therefore not met.

Regenerate the pricing with three clearly separated options: C/Lean Phase 0-only at roughly $650K-$900K, B/Balanced recommended full 36-month transformation/run around $28M-$33M including creditable Phase 0, and A/Premium around $38M-$42M if justified. Keep B within the $18M-$36M price-to-win range. Remove unsupported historical anchors, add workload/unit assumptions and published cloud/DCaaS pricing references where possible, include complete cost-stack math, and state confidence explicitly at a moderate-low level due to limited workload inventory and weak public benchmark data.
